title: Prosecutor v. Dusko Tadic — Jurisdiction and Customary IHL
citation: ICTY, IT-94-1, Appeals Chamber Decision on Jurisdiction 1995; Merits 1997; Appeals 1999
text: Tadic is the foundational case of the ICTY establishing that: (1) international armed conflict and non-international armed conflict have different treaty regimes but much customary IHL applies to both; (2) the Appeals Chamber in 1999 held that acts of individuals can be attributed to a State under an 'overall control' test (lower than Nicaragua's 'effective control') when organised armed groups act under State direction; (3) customary international law prohibits violations of Common Article 3 in non-international conflicts, and violations can amount to war crimes; (4) rape may be prosecuted as a war crime and crime against humanity. Tadic was convicted of crimes against humanity and violations of the laws and customs of war.

title: Furundzija — Torture as Jus Cogens Under International Law
text: In Furundzija (ICTY Trial Chamber, 1998), the Chamber held that the prohibition of torture has attained the status of jus cogens under international law, a peremptory norm from which no derogation is permitted. The jus cogens character entails that no treaty or domestic law can override it; treaties or domestic laws authorising or condoning torture are void. States may exercise universal jurisdiction over alleged torturers. An individual convicted of torture may not avail himself of State immunity as a personal defence. The Chamber also confirmed that rape committed by a military official in the context of an interrogation constitutes torture under international law. Furundzija is cited in virtually every subsequent international criminal law discussion of torture's peremptory status.

title: Krstic — Genocide at Srebrenica
text: Krstic (ICTY Appeals Chamber, 2004) confirmed the massacre at Srebrenica in July 1995, where approximately 7,000-8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys were killed, constituted genocide within the meaning of the Genocide Convention. The Appeals Chamber held that the killing of a substantial part of the protected group, combined with the destruction of the Srebrenica enclave as a community, satisfied the specific intent (dolus specialis) requirement of genocide. The term 'in whole or in part' was interpreted to require destruction of a 'substantial part' of the group. General Krstic was convicted as an aider and abettor to genocide. The ICJ in Bosnia v. Serbia (2007) drew extensively on ICTY findings from Krstic in determining that genocide was committed at Srebrenica.

title: R (Al-Skeini) v Secretary of State — Extraterritorial Application of ECHR in Iraq
source_name: R (Al-Skeini) v Secretary of State for Defence
citation: [2007] UKHL 26; ECtHR (Al-Skeini v UK), App. No. 55721/07, 2011
text: Al-Skeini involved deaths of Iraqi civilians at the hands of UK soldiers in Basra. The UK House of Lords and European Court of Human Rights addressed whether the ECHR applies extraterritorially. The ECtHR Grand Chamber in 2011 held that ECHR obligations apply extraterritorially when a State exercises public powers (governmental authority) over individuals, even if not in physical custody. The UK exercised jurisdiction under Article 1 ECHR over civilians killed in the context of security operations by UK forces in Basra. The Court also confirmed the 'spatial model' of jurisdiction where a State exercises effective control of an area. This case is important for the interaction between IHL and IHRL (international human rights law) in the context of extraterritorial military operations.

title: Loizidou v Turkey — Extraterritorial Human Rights Obligations
text: Loizidou v Turkey (ECtHR Grand Chamber, 1996) established that States' ECHR obligations extend to territories where they exercise 'effective control' through military force. Turkey was held responsible for violations occurring in northern Cyprus because it exercised effective overall control through its armed forces. The Court held that ECHR contracting parties are responsible for securing fundamental rights in territories under their effective control, even outside their own metropolitan territory. This principle has been applied to determine Turkey's obligations in northern Cyprus in numerous subsequent cases and influenced the development of extraterritorial human rights obligations more broadly under ICCPR and other human rights treaties.
